observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
Head of the VCEH and City Accountant to initiate reconciliation of their records and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted, to reflect in the books of accounts the correct balance of the claims from PhilHealth; and
Head of the VCEH to immediately initiate the reconciliation of records with PhilHealth to determine the status of the long unpaid claims from PhilHealth specially those claims related to CYs 2019 to 2022.

We recommended that the OCA to diligently update the Supplies Ledger Card to keep track of inventory movement and to facilitate the computation of weighted average cost on the date of issuance of supplies and materials. In such manner, related expense accounts and inventory accounts will be presented accurately in the financial statements following the prescribed Weighted Average Method of costing for supplies and materials.

Likewise, we recommended that the Inventory Committee, through the PSMD:
a. Conduct physical inventory counts of all supplies and materials in a timely manner, and submit the results using the RPCI, in compliance with Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs;
b. Submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued to the OCA to allow the latter to apply the appropriate unit cost based on the Weighted Average Method, as required under Sections 115 and 121 of the same Manual; and
c. Include in the conduct of physical count all supplies and materials issued to various offices that are still not consumed as of year-end for adjustment in the books, if any.

Moreover, we recommended that the City Mayor direct both the OCA and PSMD to:
a. Conduct regular reconciliation of the SLCs and the SCs;
b. Investigate any discrepancies identified during reconciliation; and
c. Make the necessary adjustments to ensure accuracy and consistency of records.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Head of the Property and Supply Management Division to:
Strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2022-004 on the classification of property with cost less than P50,000 as semi-expendable property; and
Investigate the existence and whereabouts of the recorded PPE in books but not included in the RPCPPE and effect the necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. Local School Board to prepare the SEF Budget in accordance with DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017, as amended by JC 1, s. 2020; and
b. Consequently, instruct the City Treasurer to transfer the total amount of P178,800,745.80 to SEF from GF.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to:
a. Require LSB and all other departments to prepare a detailed and fully accomplished PPMP in accordance with Section 4 of the Local Procurement Manual for LGUs to ensure that procurement activities are carried out as planned;
b. Direct the BAC Secretariat to consolidate all PPMPs and come up with a workable APP, ensuring that related and homogenous items are grouped and procured in a single bidding process, if warranted, and the appropriate mode of procurement is selected; and
c. Streamline the procurement process by developing a clear and efficient process flow, utilizing the APP and PPMP as the primary source document for preparing Purchase Requests.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the LCE to:
a. Direct the City Engineering Office to ensure the projects are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready to maximize the utilization of 20% DF;
b. Instruct all concerned Offices to make use of AIP in determining the projects to be implemented, and any changes in the AIP should be effected with the concurrence and approval of resolution; and
c. Require the City Planning Office to regularly assess the implementation of programs and projects to monitor and evaluate the projects as planned and to update/revise the AIP, if warranted.

We recommended the City Mayor direct the LDRRMC to properly plan and closely monitor the implementation of disaster management related PPAs to enhance preparedness, response, and recovery capabilities of the City.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the CPDO and CBO to intensify the review process to ensure that the tagging of CC P/A/Ps in the AIP and LDIP are properly made with complete details in order to provide accurate and reliable information to its intended users.

We recommended the Management to:
Expedite the utilization of the P24.411 million and P1.900 million intended for financial assistance intended for indigent individuals and procurement of multi-purpose vehicles, respectively; and
Revert immediately the unexpended balance of P205,804.00 to the National Treasury.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
Remit immediately the amount of P1,950,025.31 to the BIR to avoid the imposition of penalties, interests, and surcharges; and
Ensure that the amount remitted corresponds to the amount of taxes withheld for which it relates to prevent the over/under remittances of taxes.

We recommended the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
Cause the-immediate remittance to the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and -PhilHealth totaling P1,855,251.65 to guarantee that the City personnel/employees enjoy the full-benefits from the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth; and
Ensure that the amount of remittances corresponds to the exact amount of withheld to avoid under remittances to avoid the imposition of interests, penalties, and surcharges.

We recommended that Management direct the CAD and the CTO to periodically reconcile their records to eliminate the discrepancy to ensure accurate reporting of cash balance pursuant to Paragraph 27 of the 
IPSAS 1.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the CAD and the CTO to periodically reconcile their records to eliminate the discrepancy in order to ensure accurate reporting of cash balance pursuant to Paragraph 27 of the IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the Management adopt and fully implement the “One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances” duly authorized under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to address all the deficiencies noted.

Likewise, we also recommended the City Accountant and Property Custodian to update the lapsing schedule and the RPCPPE once the relevant data are complete and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the reported PPE and related accumulated depreciation to fairly present the financial statements.

We recommended Management to:
Comply with MOA on the duration of remittance, and consider  revision, if warranted; and
Trace all the dishonored checks related to payment of FSIF that were not previously considered as deductions in the gross remittances made to the BFP, to reflect the amount due for remittance net of any cancelled collections.

We recommended that Management require:
The City Accountant to continuously exert effort to locate the details of the remaining prior years’ unremitted withheld taxes and contributions; reconcile its records with the respective government agencies, and effect the immediate remittance of the said balances.
The City Treasurer to confer with the concerned City officials for the following issues and concerns:
Find ways and means on how to settle prior years’ obligations possibly without available supporting documents on file;
Negotiate/appeal for possible condonation of penalty/surcharge for late remittance of withheld taxes; and
Resolve the issue on payment of penalty in case the responsible withholding agent in prior years is separated from office by reason of retirement, permanent or total disability, death, permanent departure from the country or other causes.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require:
The Valenzuela City DRRMC identify effective strategies by which the management can eliminate/reduce the implementation barriers to improve in-house capacity in undertaking the LDRRM PPAs on time. Ensure the optimal use of the LDRRMF through proper identification and prioritization of PPAs to address the causes of vulnerabilities to disasters and to build resiliency among the City’s constituents in case of fortuitous events/natural hazard, casualties, including the climate change impact;
The LDRRM Officer to perform his/her responsibility on the supervision and monitoring of LDRRM fund utilization and implementation of disaster risk reduction and management PPAs. Conduct proper coordination with other concerned Department Heads to acquire the necessary funding and resources as needed to facilitate the timely implementation of all PPAs included in the LDRRM Investment Plan; and
The City Budget Officer and City Accountant to evaluate the non-moving balances of continuing appropriations covering CYs 2018 to 2022 for possible reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the GF, in case of completion or abandonment of each respective project.

We reiterated our prior year recommendations that:
The City Mayor and the City Council undertake the appropriate actions for reversion of the unexpended or excess appropriations to the unappropriated surplus of the GF, subject to re-allocation through subsequent enactment pursuant to Section 322 of RA No. 7160. Accordingly, provide this Office copies of such resolutions and/or ordinance for immediate and proper monitoring;
The CEO to closely monitor the implementation and completion of all the construction-in-progress, particularly those which remained outstanding for more than a year; and
The City Development Council (CDC), together with the City Planning and Development Office (CPDO) to improve the project prioritization process by establishing a ranking criterion to assess the urgency and importance of each project with emphasis to risks, time constraints, allocation of resources, and current operational environment.

We further enjoined the City of the following:
The City Budget Office, with the help of the CEO, to fully accomplish the utilization report specifying therein the number of extensions made, and the reasons thereof for the delay or non-implementation of projects; and
The CDC, to likewise make further analysis of the proposed projects for a better and closer budget estimate, and together with the Procurement Office, establish internal controls for the streamlining of the procurement of priority development projects to prevent unnecessary delays or non-implementation of projects.

We recommended that Management direct the:
City Planning Office to prepare a comprehensive and implementation-ready plan for the Solid Waste Management Program indicating the PPAs identified in the Annual Investment Plan with their respective budget and time-table; and
WMO in coordination with the City Budget Office and the City Planning Office to provide separate budget for the for effective monitoring of budget utilization.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Solid Waste Management Board of the City to, henceforth, prioritize early submission of ISWMP to the DENR and MMDA for review, to assure its effectiveness and efficiency as well as its consistency with the national solid waste management framework.

We recommended the GFPS to continue to observe proper planning, monitoring and evaluation for GAD programs addressing gender issues and concerns to ensure that allocation, reallocation, or reversion of funds for GAD endeavors are well spent as need be and coordinate with other national government agencies/non-government organization to avoid overlapping of projects.

Lastly, we also recommended optimum utilization of any unexpended GAD fund from completed and unimplemented PPAs and attributed programs through timely proposal and inclusion of additional PPAs and PAs for revision of the GAD Plan by way of supplemental budget duly approved by the City Council through subsequent enactment.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the LSB to utilize the funds primarily for the SEF priority projects enumerated under 
DEPED-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2020, amending JC No. 1 s. 2017.

We recommended the City Budget Officer to include in the annual budget a sufficient amount for payment of property insurance premium based on the billing statements from the GSIS. In the event of budgetary constraint, the payment of premium on calamity insurance may be charged against the LDRRMF pursuant to Section 21 of RA No. 10121.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require:
The Procurement Office and the Property and Supply Management Office to:
Strictly comply with the compulsory submission of POs within five days from perfection and NODs within 24 hours after the acceptance of deliveries to the Office of the City Auditor pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 2009-01 and 2009-02;
Stop the practice of submitting POs and NODs in batches; otherwise, institute administrative disciplinary action against officials and employees liable thereto, if warranted; and
To prepare the NODs simultaneously upon receipt and inspection of consumables/perishable items and within 24 hours submit the NOD to the Office of the City Auditor, prior to the issuance or distribution to the intended end-users/beneficiaries.
The City Accountant ensure that payments made to suppliers are supported with POs and NODs stamped “Received by” the COA within the prescribed period to ensure compliance with the requirement of COA Circulars Nos. 2009-01 and 2009-02.

We recommended that the Management require the CEO:
To strictly monitor the posting of tarpaulins/signboards at the project locations of various infrastructure projects with complete details as required under pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013; and
To inform COA within 10 days after the award of infrastructure projects or before the start of the PPAs as to the posting of the appropriate signboards and/or public notices for monitoring and validation purposes.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Management require:
The CEO to strictly monitor and supervise the completion of infrastructure PPAs within the target date of completion; and
The concerned offices involved in the planning stage should conduct detailed engineering investigations, surveys and designs for the specific project before the procurement and awarding of related contract to identify external factors that may affect the timely implementation and to avoid incurrence of undue delay on the completion of the projects.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management immediately enforce the settlement of all audit suspensions, disallowances and charges, and submission of required documents pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that the City Accountant effect the necessary reversion of stale checks totaling P4,260,452.61 to Accounts Payable for fair presentation of related accounts in the financial statements. Replacement checks should be exclusively issued to those stale checks still on hand of the City Treasurer and proper reporting of both stale and replacement checks shall be made in the Report of Check Issued. In case the stale checks were in the possession of the payees, replacement checks should await the presentation and submission of the stale checks as pre-requisite for its replacement. In the event that the stale checks were already issued to the payees, we advised the City Treasurer to immediately send written notice to the recipients of stale checks for the presentation and submission of stale checks as requirement prior to issuance of replacement check. Afterwards, secure certified copy of the DV from the Office of the City Auditor as supporting documents thereto, effect the cancellation of stale checks, issue the replacement checks, and properly record the cancelled and newly issued checks in the Report of Check Issued.
observations.managementAction: The Management adjusted all the outstanding stale checks as of December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Treasurer to continuously make follow-ups on the submission of bank credit and debit memos from concerned bank officials, especially those pertaining to prior years, to facilitate the recording of the remaining book reconciling items under the Trust Fund books. Likewise, ensure the attachment of all supporting bank credit and debit memos in the Monthly Bank Statement upon acceptance to facilitate the timely reconciliation and avoid the recurrence of unrecorded bank transactions.

We reiterate our previous year’s recommendations that:
The CAO and the PSMD continuously coordinate on the details of the PPE accounts/balances reflected in their respective records/reports to facilitate the reconciliation of the same and to submit the reconciliation report to the Office of the City Auditor, for review and verification. The Offices should also be guided by the COA Circular on the system of procedures set for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the City.
The CAO to -
Analyze the discrepancies noted between the physical count and the recorded balances of the PPE accounts to reconcile the records of their Office with that of the PSMD;
Update and maintain the PPE Ledger Cards with complete details and use a common reference number with the Property Cards kept by the PSMD; and
Work back and review entries in the PPE subsidiary ledgers with unidentified/no details; exert effort to properly include into the schedules; and effect the necessary adjustments to correct the balances of affected accounts.
The PSMD to –
Prepare and maintain Property Cards for each category of PPE and ensure that balances are tally with the RPCPPE and PPE Ledger Cards; and
Adjust the misclassified items to their proper accounts and be aware of the Circulars related to recognition of PPE to facilitate the recording in the Inventory Report.
Lastly, we highly recommended the availment and implementation of the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 21, 2020, following the prescribed guidelines and procedures in the conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), recognition of PPE items found at station and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items.

We recommended the Management to provide a separate fund for the LCPC equivalent to one percent of the IRA under the Statutory and Contractual Obligations and Budgetary Requirement to ensure compliance with the funding requirement. LCPC Fund should be utilized to the fullest amount for the continuous promotion, protection, and advocacy of children’s rights and welfare in the locality, especially for those children at risk and children in conflict with the law, and for those who need special protection.
We also advised the Management to dynamically employ ways to identify, plan and implement PPAs that will both strengthen the LCPC as well as be resilient to the present health and safety measures imposed due to the continuous surge and adverse effect of COVID-19 pandemic in the community, thereby ensuring that funds allocated will be fully utilized.

We recommended that the City Engineer closely monitor the construction of priority public infrastructure projects and enforce strict compliance with the “Revised Construction Safety Guidelines for the Implementation of Infrastructure Projects during the COVID-19 Public Health Crisis” provided for under DPWH Department Order No. 30 s. 2020, dated May 19, 2020. In such manner, implementation of identified priority projects chargeable against the 20% DP will continue without unreasonable delays amidst the surge of COVID-19 pandemic and the projected socio-economic benefits will be attained by the intended beneficiaries.
Moreover, we recommended that the City Development Council: (a) re-evaluate all public infrastructure projects chargeable against the continuing appropriations that had been left unimplemented for unreasonable number of years and recommend for the reversion of unexpended appropriations to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund,  if necessary, for subsequent enactment; (b) improve the project prioritization process by establishing ranking criteria to assess the urgency and importance of each project considering the inherent risks, time constraints, allocation of resources, and current operational environment; and (c)�refrain from issuing resolution initiating the application/realignment of unexpended continuing appropriations for other projects which is contrary to Section 322 of the Local Government Code and Section 4 of PD No. 1445.
Finally, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive, City Council, and other concerned City Officials ensure that no money shall be paid out of the local treasury except in pursuance of an appropriation law or other specific statutory authority. All unexpended balance of continuing appropriation after completion of respective projects shall be subjected to subsequent enactment through appropriation ordinance.

We recommended that the LDRRM Officer help ensure that the LDRRM Fund is efficiently utilized to the fullest amount through proper monitoring of project implementation and prioritizing programs/projects/activities taking into consideration the socio-economic condition with the continuing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System observe proper planning, monitoring, and evaluation for GAD programs addressing gender issues and concerns to ensure that allocation, reallocation, or reversion of funds for GAD endeavors are well spent as need be and coordinate with other National Government Agencies to avoid overlapping of projects.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following:
The Management prioritize the establishment of Full-Service Central Material Recovery Facilities to augment the limited number of MRFs stationed in barangays. In such manner, the drive for solid waste avoidance, volume reduction from source, and waste minimization measures as envisioned under RA 9003 will be achieved;
The City Planning Office prepare a comprehensive and implementation-ready plan for the Solid Waste Management Program indicating the projects/activities identified in the Annual Investment Plan with their respective budget and time-table; and
The Budget Officer provide a separate budget for the Waste Management Office for effective monitoring of budget utilization.

We recommended the City Engineer to regularly update the PSMD on the status of completion of all constructions in progress to facilitate the inclusion of completed agency assets in the PIF for insurance coverage with the GIF-GSIS.
We further recommended the City Budget Officer to include in the annual budget a sufficient amount for payment of property insurance premium based on the billing statements from the GSIS. In the event of budgetary constraint, the payment of premium on calamity insurance may be charged against the LDRRMF pursuant to Section 21 of RA No. 10121.

We recommended that an IAS unit be created and operationalized under the administrative supervision and control of the head of the agency in compliance with Budget Circular No. 2004-4 to ensure that the government spends within means, invests in the right priorities, and delivers measurable results for greater transparency and accountability.
We reiterate our previous recommendation that concerned City officials make representation to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the creation of an Internal Audit Service in compliance with AO No. 70, s. 2003, dated 
April 14, 2003, and its implementing guidelines under DBM Budget Circular No. 2004-04 dated March 22, 2004, on the organization and staffing of Internal Auditing Units.

We recommended the City Accounting Office and the City Treasurer’s Office to continuously monitor and remit to the GSIS, BIR, and Pag-IBIG the related contributions and taxes withheld within the prescribed period to avoid incurrence of applicable penalties and/or charges due to delay in remittance.

We recommended the City Accountant and City Treasurer to strictly adhere to Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160 requiring the City to remit the barangay shares (30%) from collection of Basic Real Property Tax, including interest thereon, directly to the barangay treasurer on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter without any lien or holdback for whatever purpose.

We recommended that the City Accountant immediately effect retrospective adjustment to the beginning balance of the Cash in Bank – LCCA, Land, and Government Equity Accounts in their respective books for the necessary correction in the accounts’ balances in the ensuing year 2022.
Henceforth, all re-assessment of Real Property and Special Education Taxes based on the just compensation of expropriated land deducted upon settlement of expropriation cases should be properly recorded under respective income accounts for fair presentation in the financial statements. It is highly recommended that the deduction for payment for the Real Property and Special Education Taxes be provided with a separate check payable to “The City Treasurer of Valenzuela City” to be received and acknowledged simultaneously by the City Treasurer through the issuance of corresponding Official Receipt as basis for recording in the books of accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management immediately enforce the settlement of all audit suspensions, disallowances and charges, and submission of required documents pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

As regards the requirement for manager’s check, the City is finding the balance between observance of strict requirements –which inadvertently burdens the taxpayers with additional procedures and cost – and acceptance of ready payment – which means immediate collection and income for the City – while maintaining mechanisms to ensure that dishonored personal checks due to insufficient funds – although the same rarely happens – could be significantly minimized.  Thus, once a taxpayer issues a personal check, which was subsequently dishonored for being Drawn Against Insufficient Funds (DAIF), that account holder will be blacklisted in the City’s system such that if they attempt to pay next time via personal check, the same will not be accepted by the City Treasurer.

observations.managementAction:
The City Government has been practicing accountability, economic, efficient and effective operations through its present system. Moreover, the City received its International Organization for Standardization (ISO) certification in June 2014 and has maintained ISO Standards ever since, which solidifies the City Government’s effort to provide quality service. In fact, the City Government has been re-certified for ISO 9001:2015 during the repeat audit, which was conducted last June 22. Moreover, the ISO Team of the City Government is actively working all year-round to ensure that the latter’s aim to improve customer satisfaction and higher level of public trust by providing quality public service in the six core offices and five support offices are met and compliant with the high international standards of ISO. 

Nevertheless, we appreciate the recommendations of this Honorable Office on the concern stated in the instant AOM. Trust that the Management will explore ways and means to comply with the said recommendations.

With respect to the four expropriation cases, which decisions are final and executory, the City Government expresses that it is ready and willing to pay the same. However, we are still awaiting the computation of back real estate taxes based on Ordinance No. 620 Series of 2019, otherwise known as the “Market Value Re-Assessment of Properties Under Expropriation Ordinance,” as well as the comments of the affected respondents/registered lot owners and their comment to said back real estate taxes.  

On the issue on escrow funds, Management still maintains its position that expropriation cases need not be backed up by escrow funds since sums of money can be put to better use by the City Government for the public good. This is 
in line with proactive sound fiscal management philosophy rather than having funds remain idle for an indefinite period of time. The City assures COA that it can meet all maturing expropriation obligations on time. 

Likewise, the City Government can project when these amounts shall be due – that is, once the appealed cases have been decided with finality. In such instances, it would be possible to set aside the needed amount for the settlement of the expropriated properties, which will go through the usual budget process. 

Management will explore ways and means for fiscal management, including formulation of strategies, intensification of collection of local taxes, monitoring of utilization of funds, as well as other administrative concerns. The City Accountant will likewise make the necessary records upon coordination with concerned offices and securing of relevant documents. The City Assessor’s Office likewise already conducted General Revision of the Fair Market Value of Real Property within the jurisdiction of Valenzuela City.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations and Management agreed that:
The City Accountant-
Ensure the setting up of RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year supported with a certified list from the City Treasurer showing the taxpayers’ names and amounts due;
Establish the beginning RPT/SET Receivables based on Taxpayer’s index card as prescribed under Section 20 Volume 1 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
Stop the practice of closing the current year’s balance of RPT/SET Receivable to prior years’ balance at year-end, instead, the closing/adjusting entry should be done at the beginning of the next calendar year.
The City Treasurer provide the City Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the name and the amount due and collectible for the current year as basis in setting up the beginning balance of RPT and SET Receivables, respectively.
The Local Chief Executive direct the City Assessor, City Treasurer, City Accountant and Information and Communication Technology Office (ICTO) Head to collaborate efforts in generating accurate/reliable data on RPT and SET receivable balances by indicating the names of taxpayers and amounts due from them, including penalties therefrom.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. 	The City Accountant effect all necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts to (a) reclassify all completed infrastructure projects still recorded under CIP accounts, including those without payment yet, to their appropriate PPE accounts; (b) compute the corresponding depreciation expense and accumulated depreciation for all 
completed projects as of December 31, 2019; (c) set-up the liability account for the unpaid costs of completed projects; and 
(d) adjust the government equity account for the unrecorded accumulated depreciation at year-end; and
b. 	The City Engineer submit to the City Accountant the list of completed infrastructure projects on monthly basis to serve as supporting documents for the latter to effect adjustment/ reclassification of CIP items to the appropriate PPE accounts.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that:
The CAO and the PSMD continuously coordinate on the details of the PPE accounts/balances reflected in their respective records/reports to facilitate the reconciliation of the same and to submit the reconciliation report to the Office of the City Auditor, for review and verification. Also, the PSMD to request from the City Engineering Office the quarterly reports on government projects/programs/activities (PPAs) for the purpose of recognizing the completed PPAs in a timely manner;
The CAO to -
Analyze the discrepancies noted between the physical count and the recorded balances of the PPE accounts to reconcile the records of their Office with that of the PSMD; and
Maintain and update the PPELC, RPLC and CIPLC with complete details and use a common reference number with the Property Cards and LRNPC kept by the PSMD.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer exert concerted efforts to account for all payments of interests on bank loans totaling P1,046,824,609.21 secured by the City in 
CY 2015 and onwards for construction of various qualifying assets. Thereafter, the City Accountant is advised to effect immediate adjustment to capitalize the determined interests as part of the depreciable cost of qualifying assets and recompute the accumulated depreciation for fair presentation in the financial statements.
Further, the City Accountant is enjoined to adopt the accrual basis of recording the interests on bank loans to comply with the underlying principle of recording all transactions and events in the financial statements of the periods to which they relate.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
The Local Chief Executive and the Local Finance Committee coordinate with the City Council for the purpose of formulating strategies to resolve the incurrence of liabilities arising from unpaid just compensation and interest on the four expropriated real estate properties totaling P401,928,272.08 million as of December 31, 2019;
The City Treasurer intensify the collection of local taxes and identify other potential sources of income to boost their collection efficiency for more funding sources; and
The City Accountant record the provision for just compensation obligations including interests thereon amounting to P401,928,272.08 in the books of accounts and present the facts in the Notes to Financial Statement.
Henceforth, the Management is advised to defer from expropriating property until such time that the City is capable to settle its existing just compensation obligations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that the 20% DF is optimally utilized for the projects, programs and activities to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management which shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditure of the City.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Heads of the CSWD and LCPC jointly implement all PPAs identified in the approved LCPC Plan to fully utilize the equivalent amount of one percent of the IRA as mandated under RA No. 9344. As such, the LCPC will be endowed to uphold their advocacy on child protection for their physical, moral, spiritual, intellectual and social well-being, especially for those children at risk and children in conflict with the law.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City and the 
GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) strictly adhere to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 for the timely submission of the GAD Accomplishment Report to the OCA.

We recommended and Management agreed that the GFPS:
Observe proper GAD planning and budgeting to ensure that allocation of funds for GAD endeavors are well spent and coordinate with other NGAs to avoid overlapping of projects; and
Strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs as required under JMC No. 2013-01 and conduct regular coordination with the implementing offices to ensure that programs are undertaken within the target period.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Management  furnish the GSIS with a copy of the latest inventory report of its insurable properties for the computation of the amounts of insurance premium; and appropriate funds in the annual budget for the billed costs of insurance premiums for all insurable property of the City. Also, we advise the PSMD to prepare and submit the PIF in compliance with COA Circular 
No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer prepare the required LDRRMFIP with the details of each PPA intended for disaster mitigation, prevention and 
preparedness pursuant to 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. The LDRRMF, including the five-year unexpended balance in the Special Trust Fund, should be fully utilized in strict observance of the schedule of implementation of identified PPAs to fully achieve the fund’s objectives as envisioned by the law.

13.7 We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant and City Treasurer  strictly adhere with Section 271 (d) of RA No. 7160 requiring the City to remit the barangay shares (30%) from collection of Basic RPT, including interests thereon, directly to the barangay treasurer on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter without any lien or holdback for whatever purpose.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Prioritize the construction of the Central MRF in order to augment the limited number of full-service MRFs stationed in barangays;
Provide technical assistance to barangays by monitoring that the MRFs located within the barangays are properly used and well maintained; and
Require the City Budget Office to separate the budget for the SWM operation for proper monitoring and evaluation of the reasonableness thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the City Accountant to verify the recording of taxes withheld/remitted and cross-check journal entries with the Statement of Remittances to determine the causes of abnormal balances or over remittances; and
Advice the City Accountant and City Treasurer to strictly comply with the withholding taxes and remittance thereof within ten days following the end of each month as required under BIR Revenue MC No. 23-2012 dated February 14, 2012 and BIR Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 dated May 17, 1998 to avoid incurrence of penalties and surcharges pursuant to Section 272 of the National Internal Revenue Code (NIRC).

We recommended and Management agreed to require the CAO to determine the composition of the prior years’ obligations and remit immediately to the GSIS the total amount including the current and past due payables to GSIS. In case the payees could no longer be identified, we advise the City government to make representations with GSIS to discuss settlement on the unremitted shares/payments.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the CAO to determine the composition of prior years’ obligations and remit immediately to HDMF the current and past due payables, to avoid imposition of further penalty to the City. We also advised the management to make representations with the said agency to discuss settlement the unremitted contributions.
We reiterated our previous recommendation that the concerned City officials make representations to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the establishment of the Internal Audit Service/Unit in the City.

We recommended strict compliance with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-002 requiring submission of copies of contracts and POs within five days from perfection and to submit the IARs within 24 hours after the acceptance of deliveries of goods and services.

The CAO review and analyze the book RIs labeled as Errors (CM/DM), verify its actual descriptions and/or nature and correspondingly adjust the same in the books of accounts to ensure correctness of the account balances.

This aimed to boost the collections of RPT and SET receivables from calendar years 2014 to 2019 and condoning earlier years’ unpaid taxes. Such one-time amnesty program is also launched to shorten the cleansing process of old-aged accounts and to facilitate the rendition of accurate receivables from RPT and SET. 

After the amnesty period, management intends to institute appropriate civil remedies for the collections of delinquent accounts from those who did not avail of the program.
observations.reason:
The completeness and correctness of RPT/SET receivables provided by the City Treasurer to the City Accounting Office for recording could not be ascertained.

The reports generated by the RPT System of RPT/SET Receivables by IT Officials is unreliable due to unreliable data in the system.

The cleansing of old-aged accounts is still in process.

We recommended that the CAO adopt the revised estimated useful life of assets in the computation of depreciation of the PPE accounts in compliance with COA Circular No. 2003-007.

We recommended that the Management require the In-Charge of Office (ICO), City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) to strictly compel the AOs to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period and support the same with proper documents.

We recommended that the Management require the BAC or concerned officials to submit the copies of the awarded contracts and required supporting documents within the prescribed period pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001, to facilitate the review and evaluation of the same and to avoid subjecting the responsible officials to administrative disciplinary action.

We recommended that the Management require the BAC to check if the contracts and supporting documents were properly accomplished and/or filled up before submitting to the Office of the Auditor for review and evaluation to ensure the validity and propriety of the same. We likewise advise the BAC to review carefully the entries found in the various PRs and POs and determine if the exact similarities of the same were done in good faith or there were lapses in the process.
We recommended that the BAC strictly conform with the provisions of the RIRR in the formulation of the contract for infrastructure projects to ensure the legality and validity of the same.

We recommended that the Management  require the CEO to  (a) prepare the ABC of the project in conformity with DO No. 22 issued by DPWH; (b) demand from the contractors the refund of the  overpayments  made due to the said overestimated ABCs to be utilized  for other programmed/beneficial  projects of the City; and (c) require the CAO to make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts and the Property Officer to adjust/correct the costs of the projects in the Property Plant and Equipment Inventory Report, if refunds, were made.

We recommended that the Management comply strictly with the pertinent provisions of P.D. 1445 and other relevant COA issuances on the notification and submission of documents for inspection and for validation and post-audit purposes.

We recommended that the Management require (a) the responsible officials implementing the various PPAs funded by the NGAs to utilize the funds for the intended purposes/beneficiaries and to abide strictly to what were stipulated in the respective MOAs specifically on the liquidation of the funds; (b) the CAO to exert extra effort to look for the documents supporting the non-moving balances and ascertain the reasons why the same remained in the books for so long, and coordinate with the offices/department concerned the reasons for the projects’ non/partial implementations; (c) the implementing office/department to coordinate with the CHD to facilitate the provision of QAC  for the immediate implementation of the  NTP; and  (d) the CAO to reconcile their records with the NGAs books of accounts and request for the utilization of the remaining balances for other related projects also beneficial for the City’s constituents, if not, remit the remaining cash to the concerned NGA’s so that the accounts can  be closed in both  accounting records.

We reiterated  our prior year’s recommendation that the City Accountant and the ICO – CTO exert extra effort to effect the timely release of the 30 percent share from the basic RPT to the 33 Barangays of Valenzuela City so that these barangays can promptly implement their respective PPAs.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Management require (a) the City personnel to withdraw cash advance to cover the costs of seminars and travels, to ensure that the transactions are fully documented; and (b) advise the CAO not to process claims/payments for travel expenses that were directly charged to expense.

We recommended that the City Accounting Office strictly require that all transactions are properly supported with complete documentations to establish the propriety and validity of the claims and/or payment.

We recommended that the Barangay Chairmen of the City require the Barangay Treasurers (BTs) to facilitate the consolidation of the paid disbursement vouchers and supporting documents to ensure on-time submission of PBCs.
We further recommended that the CAO advise the League of the Barangays–Valenzuela City to require and encourage the Barangay Chairmen to submit the transaction documents within the prescribed period to ensure: (a) an updated recording in the books of accounts; (b) non-suspension/holding of encashment of checks; (c) on time and/or continuous payment of salaries and delivery of services to their constituents; and (d) non-imposition of disciplinary actions to erring barangay officials.

15.10 We recommended that the Management (a) utilize the PCF in the procurement of drugs and medicines in accordance with the pertinent provisions of PHIC issuances to ensure the maximum use of the same and the availability of more drugs and medicines for distribution to qualified beneficiaries/patients; and (b) regularly include in their procurement of drugs and medicines, those for the treatment of other medical conditions to ensure sufficient supplies of the same, thus helping the indigent patients from the burden of costly medicines.

We recommended that the Management advise the Bids and  Awards Committee to stop resorting to alternative mode of procurement unless all the conditions under which mode may be availed were justified; and  strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of the IRR in the procurement of drugs and medicines not only to ensure the validity and propriety of the transactions but also an opportunity to avail for the most advantageous prices.

We recommended that the  Management require the CAO to (a) determine the composition of the prior years’ obligations and remit immediately to the GSIS the total amount including the  current and past due payables to GSIS; and (b) adhere strictly with the GSIS regulations on the prescribed period of remittance of the employees’ and employer’s contribution to avoid payment of interests, non-granting of loans to officials and employees and delays in the processing of claims or forfeiture of benefits due to the employees.

We recommended that the Management require the CAO to exert extra effort to locate the details of the prior years’ unremitted withheld taxes; reconcile its records with the BIR and effect the immediate remittance of the said balances, to ensure additional income for the national government to finance its programmed PPAs.

19.12 We recommended  that the Management require the Head, Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) to evaluate the performance of the City personnel especially those who have (a) civil service eligibilities, (b) board examinations, and (c) worked  with the City government for five years; and recommend  for  regularization and promotion, if warranted, to encourage/promote efficiency and self-confidence; and ensure that accountability, responsibly and liability will strictly be enforced and in place.

We recommended that the Management require the (a) Gender and Development Focal Points System Council and concerned officials to include implementable and beneficial PPAs in the GAD Plans and Budgets to ensure the optimum utilization of the GAD Funds; and (b)   concerned officials to amend the title of the GAD Plan to GAD Plan and Budget to conform and/or comply with existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Management update and illustrate through an enacted ordinance the revised graduated business tax rates reflecting such escalation clauses in a precise manner to establish a legal basis for the assessment and collection of the business taxes.

We recommended that the Management require the BPLO to provide the Office of the Auditor the assessments/computations of the business taxes and licenses of the five business establishments to enable this Office to verify and evaluate the correctness and accuracy of the paid taxes.

We recommended that the Management revisit anew the provisions of the Revenue Code of the City and the Ordinance on the regulatory fees and charges with reflection to the fast growing commercialization of business establishments within the City.

We recommended that the Management require the Local School Board (LSB) to appropriate the SEF to the  priority programs, projects and activities enumerated in Sections 100 (c) and 272 of the Local Government Code to ensure the propriety and legality of the  OMOE  account.

We recommended that the Management  (a) require the LSB to utilize the SEF strictly for priority programs, projects and activities enumerated in Sections 100 (c) and 272 of the Local Government Code to ensure the propriety and validity of the transactions; and (b) disallow transactions  in the SEF which  have no legal basis.

We recommended that the Management require the Property Officer  of the LSB to notify the City of the deliveries of meals and snacks before consumption and require the concerned officers who requisitioned the same to include in the Purchase Request and other related documents the details of the meals and snacks to be delivered, for validation and monitoring purposes. The City Accounting Office is also advised to avoid processing the related transactions that have not undergone inspection by the GSO prior to consumption.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management require: (a) the concerned officials to conduct inventory of the vehicles, determine the present running condition and evaluate if the costs of repairs undertaken on the said vehicles had already surpassed the reasonable/economical amounts; and (b) the concerned officials to prepare a separate report on the conducted inventory and evaluation, if necessary undertake proper disposal thereof and inform the Auditor accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The management informed that they have already stopped the repair of fully depreciated equipment.
observations.reason: The PSMD Head has not yet submitted a separate report on the conducted inventory and evaluation of fully depreciated equipment. | The PSMD Head has not yet submitted a separate report on the conducted inventory and evaluation of fully depreciated equipment.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management provide sufficient escrow funds for the payment of the costs of expropriated land and other incidental expenses to ensure the availability of funds once the cases have been decided in the proper courts.
observations.managementAction: The management informed that they always have available funds for the payment of the settled expropriated lands.
observations.reason: Although the City has not maintained an escrow funds, they have already paid out and settled all the resolved/awarded expropriated lands. | Although the City has not maintained an escrow funds, they have already paid out and settled all the resolved/awarded expropriated lands.

observations.recommendation: The Accounting Office prepared the necessary adjusting entries to correct the misclassification in the expense accounts affected. Moreover, the allotment for each type of expenses should be properly reviewed by the City Budget Office to eliminate the practice of lump sum allotment to OMOE account.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office had prepared various journal entry vouchers to correct the expenses erroneously recorded under the OMOE.

observations.recommendation:
The members of the Local School Board, in the preparation of School Board budget give priority to programs, activities and projects enumerated in Sections 100 (c), 272 of the Local Government Code and Joint Circular No. 01, s. of 1998 of the DECS-DBM-DILG and therefore must utilize the proceeds of the SEF only for such purposes prescribed by law.
The expenses incurred for the payment of the above-cited medicines, dental, medical and laboratory supplies and other non-SEF expenses be charged against the General Fund of the City.

The payment of other bonuses and allowances for CY 2014 be reimbursed to SEF in compliance with the above-cited regulations.
observations.managementAction: The management justified that medicines were procured to cater the health related needs of the students through the use of mobile clinics.
observations.reason: The purchase of medicines using SEF was already stopped but there were still ineligible expenses incurred by the City, one of the issues in our report. | The purchase of medicines using SEF was already stopped but there were still ineligible expenses incurred by the City, one of the issues in our report.

observations.recommendation: Management prepare and submit a Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the Office of the Supervising Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month for the purpose of monitoring the utilization of the Fund in accordance with the guidelines set forth under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction: Management answered that for the purpose of monitoring the utilization of the LDRRM Fund in accordance with the guidelines set forth under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, their office will furnish a Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to be submitted on or before the 15th day after the end of each month to your office starting January 2015.
observations.reason: The City Accounting Office prepared Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF but the same were submitted on a quarterly basis instead on a monthly basis. | The City Accounting Office prepared Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF but the same were submitted on a quarterly basis instead on a monthly basis.

observations.recommendation: Quarterly reconciliations be made between the balances per cashbooks and subsidiary ledgers to immediately resolve any discrepancy that may be due to erroneous recording/posting, unrecorded disbursements and thereafter adjust the same with the use of Reconciliation Statements. Furthermore, exert extra effort to trace these variances mentioned above that could be attributed to the reconciling items of the previous years.
observations.managementAction: The CAO and CTO is already conducting reconciliation of their respective books and records.

observations.recommendation: The OIC- City Accounting Office analyze/verify the details of the unknown/unidentified CIP-Agency Assets balance of P12,633,141 under the Special Education Fund and maintain Subsidiary Ledger  and Constructions in Project Ledger Card (CIPLC) for each project undertaken.
observations.managementAction: The CAO had  already transferred and recorded the completed projects in the appropriate PPE accounts and had already maintained the necessary subsidiary ledgers.

observations.recommendation: The accounting personnel concerned analyze   and identify the causes of the discrepancies and take up the necessary adjusting entries to correct the misstatement of the Cash in Bank account.
observations.managementAction: The discrepancy/variance between the accounting books and bank records were already identified because of the availability of Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements.

observations.recommendation: Reiteration of our previous audit observation that the City Accountant reclassify the said expenses/services to their proper accounts and strictly observe/follow the updated description of accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2004-008 dated September 20, 2004 for a proper presentation of its financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The payments of the said expenses were already classified to the appropriate accounts.

observations.recommendation: All titles to land owned by and/or donated to the City should be transferred and registered in the name of the City as provided for under Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992.  It is likewise recommended that the necessary budget for the transfer/registration and related expenses should be determined and appropriated in the next year’s annual budget.
observations.managementAction: Under the PPSAS, the assets are recognized because of its economic benefits and/or use thereof, thus, with or without titles, the same were already taken up/reflected in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation: Refund of all the honoraria received by the concerned officials and employees for their memberships in various committees for the CYs 2012 and 2013 and likewise for the excess honoraria received in CY 2011.
observations.managementAction: The Director, Local Government Sector had sustained the issued Notice of Disallowance.
observations.reason: Only officials and employees who have either resigned or retired have refunded the unauthorized honoraria. | Only officials and employees who have either resigned or retired have refunded the unauthorized honoraria.
